title: Inversion Sorting
description: Interactive problem: A hidden permutation of 1..n must be sorted by reversing subarrays. After each reversal [i, j], the grader returns the total number of inversions. If the number becomes 0, the permutation is sorted and the program must terminate. You may make at most 4n operations. Your program should read n, then repeatedly output i j, flush, and read the new inversion count. Use the inversion count change to guide your next move.

examples.explanation: The program prints queries `i j`, flushes, and reads inversion counts in response. It stops when the count becomes 0.

constraints: You can make at most 4n operations
After each printed pair i j (1 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ n), read the new inversion count
Stop immediately when the inversion count becomes 0

mcqChain.questionText: For permutation [2, 1, 3], how many inversions are there?
mcqChain.explanation: Only (2,1) forms an inversion. Others are in order.

wrongExplanations.2: Count each pair carefully; only one pair is out of order.
wrongExplanations.3: There are 3 pairs total but most are in order.

mcqChain.hint: List all pairs (i<j) and check if a[i] > a[j].

mcqChain.questionText: Starting from [3, 1, 2], reverse subarray [1, 2] (i.e., swap the first two elements). What is the new inversion count?
mcqChain.explanation: New array [1,3,2] inversions: (3,2) only → 1.

wrongExplanations.2: Re-evaluate after reversal; (3,1) disappears because 1 moves to front.
wrongExplanations.3: There are fewer inversions after fixing an inverted adjacent pair.

mcqChain.hint: Compute inversions before and after reversal.

mcqChain.questionText: If you reverse a subarray of length 1, what happens to the inversion count?

options.label: It always increases by 1.
It always decreases by 1.
It stays the same.
It may increase or decrease depending on the position.

mcqChain.explanation: Reversing a single element does nothing to the array, so count is unchanged.

wrongExplanations.0: No elements are swapped, so no pair status changes.
wrongExplanations.1: No swap happens; count cannot decrease automatically.

mcqChain.hint: Reversing length 1 is a no-op.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider two adjacent elements a[i], a[i+1]. If you reverse [i, i+1], how does the inversion count change?
mcqChain.diagram: Only the relative order of these two changes; all other pairs remain same.

options.label: It changes by +1 if a[i] < a[i+1], by -1 if a[i] > a[i+1].
It always decreases by 1.
It always increases by 1.
It changes by ±2 depending on the pair.

mcqChain.explanation: Swapping a non-inverted adjacent pair creates one inversion (+1). Swapping an inverted pair fixes one (-1).

wrongExplanations.1: If the pair was already in order, swapping makes it worse.
wrongExplanations.3: Only one pair changes status, so ±1 not ±2.

mcqChain.hint: Only one pair’s order flips.

mcqChain.questionText: Why can reversing [i, i+1] be used as a probe to learn local order in this interactive task?

options.label: Because the grader reveals the exact pair values after each move.
Because the inversion count change tells if the adjacent pair was inverted or not.
Because reversing any subarray reports positions of elements.
Because the inversion count always becomes 0 after swapping a pair.

mcqChain.explanation: A ±1 change reveals whether the adjacent pair was out of order.

wrongExplanations.0: The grader only returns the total inversion count, not values.
wrongExplanations.3: Swapping one pair rarely sorts the whole array.

mcqChain.hint: Relate ±1 change to local inversion status.

mcqChain.questionText: A naive idea: repeatedly reverse random subarrays hoping to reduce inversions. Why is this unreliable?

options.label: It might get stuck increasing inversions without feedback.
Random reversals always reduce by at least 1.
Random reversals are forbidden by the problem.
Random reversals do not change inversion count.

mcqChain.explanation: Random choices can increase inversions and waste the limited 4n moves.

wrongExplanations.1: A reversal can increase the inversion count.
wrongExplanations.3: Reversals generally do change inversions.

mcqChain.hint: Think about the move limit and unpredictability.

mcqChain.questionText: Swapping adjacent elements reveals local order. What’s a safe way to use this without making things worse?

options.label: Swap adjacent, keep it regardless of inversion change.
Swap adjacent; if inversion count increases, immediately swap back.
Never swap adjacent elements.
Swap only non-adjacent indices to avoid changing local order.

mcqChain.explanation: Undo the swap if it hurts. This uses feedback to avoid worsening.

wrongExplanations.0: Keeping harmful swaps accumulates more inversions.
wrongExplanations.3: Non-adjacent reversals obscure local effects more.

mcqChain.hint: Use the sign of the change to decide whether to keep the swap.

mcqChain.questionText: Why is probing adjacent pairs in sequence (left to right, then right to left) sensible under a 4n cap?

options.label: Each full sweep costs O(n) moves and tends to move misplaced elements toward correct sides.
One sweep always fully sorts the array.
Sweeps never increase inversion count overall.
Sweeps reveal exact positions of all values.

mcqChain.explanation: Sweeps use local improvements and cost linear moves per pass.

wrongExplanations.3: We only get global counts, not exact positions.

mcqChain.hint: Balance progress per pass with move budget.

mcqChain.questionText: Suppose you swap positions i and i+1 and the inversion count decreases by 1. What should you do next?

options.label: Immediately swap them back.
Keep the swap and move on to the next adjacent pair.
Reverse a large subarray to speed up.
Terminate the program.

mcqChain.explanation: A -1 change means you fixed a local inversion; keep it.

wrongExplanations.0: Undoing a helpful move loses progress.
wrongExplanations.2: Large reversals are risky without clear feedback.

mcqChain.hint: Use the sign of the change.

mcqChain.questionText: If the inversion count increases by 1 after swapping [i, i+1], what’s the correct response?

options.label: Keep it, since any change is progress.
Undo the swap by reversing [i, i+1] again.
Reverse [1, n] to compensate.
Ignore the change and continue.

mcqChain.explanation: Undoing prevents making the sequence worse.

wrongExplanations.3: Ignoring makes errors accumulate.

mcqChain.hint: We want non-increasing inversion counts across actions.

mcqChain.questionText: Why is an initial reversal [1, 1] (no-op) useful at the start of the interaction?

options.label: It magically sorts half the array.
It reveals the current inversion count without changing the permutation.
It reduces inversions by 1 for free.
It is required by the grader before any other operations.

mcqChain.explanation: Reversing length 1 returns the current inversion count with no side effects.

wrongExplanations.3: Not a rule; it’s just a harmless way to read the count.

mcqChain.hint: We need an initial count to compare future changes.

mcqChain.questionText: A left-to-right pass checks pairs (1,2), (2,3), ..., (n-1,n). How many operations can one such pass cost in the worst case (including undoing harmful swaps)?
mcqChain.explanation: Each pair may need 1 swap (kept) or 2 swaps (try + undo). Worst case is 2 per pair.

wrongExplanations.0: If many pairs are already correct, it's n-1, but worst-case needs undo.
wrongExplanations.2: That many operations would require re-checking each pair many times.

mcqChain.hint: Count try and possible undo per adjacent pair.

mcqChain.questionText: Why perform both a left-to-right and a right-to-left pass before checking the stopping condition again?

options.label: To move misplaced elements in both directions efficiently within the move budget.
Because the grader requires alternating passes.
Because a single direction never reduces inversions.
To avoid flushing output too often.

mcqChain.explanation: Alternating directions helps relocate elements faster under limited moves.

wrongExplanations.2: A single direction can reduce inversions; alternating helps more.

mcqChain.hint: Think about elements that need to move both left and right.

mcqChain.questionText: Set up interactive I/O: which import is necessary for flushing and reading stdin efficiently?

options.label: import sys
import os
from collections import deque
import threading

mcqChain.explanation: We need sys for stdout.flush() and stdin.

wrongExplanations.1: os isn't needed for basic I/O here.
wrongExplanations.2: deque isn't used in this approach.

mcqChain.hint: Use Python's standard streams.

mcqChain.questionText: Define a helper to perform a reversal: print i and j, flush, and read the new inversion count. Which is correct?

options.label:
def do(i,j):
    print(i, j)
    resp = input().strip()
    return resp

mcqChain.explanation: Flush, read line, handle EOF, and parse int are all handled.

wrongExplanations.1: Returns a string and misses flush; also no EOF handling.
wrongExplanations.3: No strip/EOF check; can crash on empty lines.

mcqChain.hint: Ensure flushing and robust reading.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
def do(i,j):
    print(i, j)
    sys.stdout.flush()
    resp = sys.stdin.readline().strip()
    if not resp:
        sys.exit(0)
    v = int(resp)
    return v
mcqChain.codeLabel: Helper to perform a move and read inversion count

mcqChain.questionText: Start main: read n from stdin safely. Which snippet is best?

options.label:
def main():
    n = int(input())

def main():
    n = int(sys.stdin.read())

def main():
    n = sys.stdin.readline()

mcqChain.explanation: Reads one line, checks empty, and parses int.

wrongExplanations.1: input() can work, but mixing with readline may be problematic.
wrongExplanations.3: Doesn't parse to int and ignores empty input.

mcqChain.hint: Be consistent with sys.stdin and handle empty input.

mcqChain.questionText: Before making comparisons, fetch the initial inversion count using a no-op move. Which is correct?

options.label:
ops = 0
inv = do(1, 1)
ops += 1

ops = 0
inv = 0

ops = 1
inv = do(0, 0)

ops = 0
inv = do(n, n)
ops += n

mcqChain.explanation: Reverse [1,1] to read the count and increment ops by 1.

wrongExplanations.1: We don't know the initial inversion count.

mcqChain.hint: Use a harmless move to get the count.
mcqChain.codeLabel: Initialize ops and read initial inversion count

mcqChain.questionText: Implement the loop guard: continue while not sorted and within the 4n move budget.

options.label: while inv != 0 and ops < 4*n:
while ops <= 4*n:
while True:
while inv > 0 or ops < 4*n:

mcqChain.explanation: We need both: not sorted and within budget.

wrongExplanations.1: May loop after sorting; also allows exactly 4n+ moves if not careful.
wrongExplanations.3: Using OR can loop even if one condition fails.

mcqChain.hint: Both conditions must hold to proceed.

mcqChain.questionText: Write the left-to-right pass over adjacent pairs with try/undo logic.
mcqChain.diagram: Check (1,2), (2,3), ..., (n-1,n). Undo if inversion count increases.
mcqChain.explanation: Correctly iterates 1..n-1, tries swap, keeps if helpful, otherwise undoes.

wrongExplanations.1: 0-based indices are invalid for the interactive indices; also lacks undo and ops.
wrongExplanations.3: Blindly applies swaps without undoing increases.

mcqChain.hint: Remember 1-based indices and undo logic.

mcqChain.questionText: Before starting the right-to-left pass, add a quick break if done or out of moves.

options.label:
if inv == 0 or ops >= 4*n:
            break

if inv != 0 and ops < 4*n:
            continue

if inv == 0 and ops == 4*n:
            break
pass

mcqChain.explanation: Short-circuit if already sorted or at budget.

wrongExplanations.1: This just continues the loop without moving to the next phase cleanly.
wrongExplanations.3: Missing the necessary guard.

mcqChain.hint: Mirror the main loop guard here.

mcqChain.questionText: Write the right-to-left pass over adjacent pairs with the same try/undo logic.
mcqChain.explanation: Iterates i=n-1..1, tries swap, keeps if helpful, undoes otherwise.

wrongExplanations.1: Indices reversed (i,i-1) doesn't match our do() expectation and order.
wrongExplanations.3: No undo if the swap is harmful.

mcqChain.hint: Mirror the forward pass with reversed index order.

mcqChain.questionText: Finish the main function and add the usual Python entry point. Which is correct?

options.label:
return

main()

mcqChain.explanation: A simple return at end and a guarded entry point are standard.

wrongExplanations.3: Calling main() unguarded can cause issues if imported.
wrongExplanations.2: Using pass is fine but less explicit than return.

mcqChain.hint: Use the common __main__ pattern.

mcqChain.questionText: Assuming the grader reports initial inversion count 3 after the no-op, and the first adjacent swap [1,2] returns 2, what should the code do?

options.label: Keep the swap and proceed to (2,3).
Undo the swap immediately.
Reverse [1, n] next.
Terminate early.

mcqChain.explanation: 2 < 3 means the swap helped; keep it.

wrongExplanations.1: Undo only when the count increases.
wrongExplanations.3: Not sorted yet; count is not 0.

mcqChain.hint: Compare new_inv to inv.

mcqChain.questionText: Mid-run, suppose a trial swap gives new_inv = inv + 1. How many total operations does this pair consume (including undo)?
mcqChain.explanation: One for the trial swap and one to undo it.

wrongExplanations.1: You must undo to avoid worsening the sequence.
wrongExplanations.3: We only need one undo per bad trial.

mcqChain.hint: Count both the trial and the undo.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the worst-case number of operations per complete forward+backward sweep?

options.label: O(n)
O(log n)

mcqChain.explanation: Each adjacent pair may cost up to 2 operations per pass: linear overall per pass.

wrongExplanations.2: That's per many passes; a single sweep is linear.
wrongExplanations.1: You touch many pairs, not constant work.

mcqChain.hint: Sum cost across adjacent pairs.

mcqChain.questionText: When should the program terminate immediately during interaction?

options.label: When the inversion count equals 0 after any move.
When the inversion count is less than n.
After exactly 4n moves.
When the inversion count stops changing for one pass.

mcqChain.explanation: A count of 0 means the permutation is sorted.

wrongExplanations.2: Stop earlier if sorted to avoid extra moves.
wrongExplanations.3: Plateaus can occur before completion.

mcqChain.hint: The grader signals completion via the count.

mcqChain.questionText: Edge cases: What if n = 1?

options.label: Do a no-op to read count, then immediately exit since it's already sorted.
Perform arbitrary reversals to confirm.
Reverse [1,1] until the grader returns 0.
It's impossible to handle n = 1.

mcqChain.explanation: Single element has 0 inversions; the no-op reveals it.

wrongExplanations.3: n = 1 is trivial.

mcqChain.hint: A single element is already sorted.

reasoningChain.question: What is an inversion and how does a local adjacent reversal affect it?
reasoningChain.insight: Swapping adjacent elements changes the inversion count by ±1 depending on whether the pair was inverted.

reasoningChain.question: How can we safely probe local order without making the permutation worse?
reasoningChain.insight: Attempt a swap; if inversion count increases, immediately undo it.

reasoningChain.question: How do we use sequential adjacent probes to make progress under a move limit?
reasoningChain.insight: Perform left-to-right and right-to-left passes to reduce inversions with linear moves per pass.

reasoningChain.question: How do we interface with the interactive grader correctly?
reasoningChain.insight: Print moves, flush, read the new inversion count, and stop when it reaches 0.

reasoningChain.question: Why perform an initial no-op reversal?
reasoningChain.insight: To obtain the starting inversion count without altering the hidden permutation.

reasoningChain.question: When should the loop stop?
reasoningChain.insight: Stop when inversion count is 0 or when reaching the 4n move budget.
